<var-list> ::= <var> | <var> "," <var-list>

1. A <document> contains one asserted always-holding custom, marked by "A", and one asked regularity, marked by "Q"; "#" separates them.
2. In a <case>, the variables inside "[" and "]" are the freely chosen starting things, universally quantified in their listed order.
3. An <assignment> of the form r=*(a,b) means: perform the combining action with ordered first participant a and second participant b, and name the result r.
4. Assignments inside "{" and "}" are performed from left to right.
5. A <res> denotes the result named by its assignment; a valid encoding assigns each result name at most once and uses a result name only after it has been assigned in that case.
6. "~(a,b)" states, inside "A", that a and b always come out the same; inside "Q", it asks whether a and b must always come out the same.

<document> ::= <assertion><question>
<assertion> ::= "A[" <start-list> ":" <definition-list> "=>" <equation> "]"
<question> ::= "Q[" <start-list> ":" <definition-list> "=>" <equation> "]"
<start-list> ::= <start> | <start> "," <start-list>
<definition-list> ::= <definition> | <definition> ";" <definition-list>
<definition> ::= <result> ":=" "C(" <term> "," <term> ")"
<equation> ::= <term> "=" <term>
<term> ::= <start> | <result>
<start> ::= "x" <positive>
<result> ::= "r" <positive>
<positive> ::= <nonzero> | <nonzero><digits>
<digits> ::= <digit> | <digit><digits>
<digit> ::= "0" | <nonzero>
<nonzero> ::= "1" | "2" | "3" | "4" | "5" | "6" | "7" | "8" | "9"

1. `<document>` is one asserted custom followed by one asked regularity.
2. `A[...]` asserts that, for all freely chosen starting things listed before `:`, the definitions after `:` are performed in order and the equation after `=>` always holds.
3. `Q[...]` asks, without asserting, whether for all freely chosen starting things listed before `:`, the definitions after `:` are performed in order and the equation after `=>` must always hold.
4. `x1`, `x2`, etc. name freely chosen starting things. In this encoding, `x1` is the first named starting thing and `x2` is the second.
5. `r1`, `r2`, etc. name intermediate results defined by the ordered definitions within the same bracketed block.
6. `C(a,b)` means one performance of the combining action with first participant `a` and second participant `b`; the order of `a,b` is significant.
7. `a=b` means the two denoted things come out the same in the relevant sense.
8. Within a valid block, listed starts are distinct, each result is defined at most once, and any result used in a definition or equation has already been defined earlier in that same block.

<assertion> ::= "ASSERT" <clause>

<clause> ::= "FORALL" "(" <idlist> ")" "{" <body> "}"
<idlist> ::= <id> | <id> "," <idlist>
<body> ::= <equality> | <definition> ";" <body>
<definition> ::= <id> ":=" "(" <id> "*" <id> ")"
<equality> ::= "EQ" "(" <id> "," <id> ")"
<id> ::= <letter> | <letter> <tail>
<tail> ::= <char> | <char> <tail>
<char> ::= <letter> | <digit>
<letter> ::= "a" | "b" | "c" | "d" | "e" | "f" | "g" | "h" | "i" | "j" | "k" | "l" | "m" | "n" | "o" | "p" | "q" | "r" | "s" | "t" | "u" | "v" | "w" | "x" | "y" | "z"

1. A <program> contains exactly one asserted custom and exactly one asked regularity.
2. "FORALL" "(" <idlist> ")" "{" <body> "}" means the listed identifiers are freely chosen starting things, universally quantified.
3. A definition x:=(y*z) records one ordered combining action whose first participant is y and whose second participant is z; the result is named x.
4. Definitions in a body are read from left to right. Each right-hand identifier must be either a starting identifier from the enclosing FORALL list or a result identifier defined earlier in the same body.
5. EQ(x,y) states that x and y come out the same. Under ASSERT it is claimed always to hold; under ASK it is only queried.

1. A <story> has one asserted universal custom, marked by "ASSERT", and one merely asked universal regularity, marked by "ASK".
2. In a <block>, "forall(...)" lists the freely chosen starting things; their order records first, second, third, and so on.
3. A <step> records one performed combining action. "op(a,b)" means combine participant a with participant b in that order.
4. Steps inside "{" and "}" are evaluated from left to right; the left side result name stores the result of that step.
5. An <equality> after "=>" names the two things claimed or asked to come out the same after the listed steps.
6. No commutativity, associativity, identity law, or other property of "op" is assumed by the notation.

2. In a <block>, "forall(...)" lists the freely chosen starting things; their order records first, second, third, and so on.

response:
GRAMMAR:
```
<story> ::= <custom> <question>

<custom> ::= "CUSTOM" <signal_list> <procedure_list> "ASSERT" <equivalence_list>
<question> ::= "QUESTION" <signal_list> <procedure_list> "ASK" <equivalence_list>

<signal_list> ::= "SIGNALS" <signal_name> ("," <signal_name>)*
<signal_name> ::= <identifier>

<procedure_list> ::= "PROCEDURES" <procedure> (";" <procedure>)*
<procedure> ::= <result_name> "=" <input_signal> "THROUGH" <input_signal>
<result_name> ::= <identifier>
<input_signal> ::= <signal_name> | <result_name>

<equivalence_list> ::= <equivalence> ("AND" <equivalence>)*
<equivalence> ::= <signal_name> "EQ" <input_signal>

<identifier> ::= <letter> (<letter> | <digit>)*
<letter> ::= "a" | ... | "z" | "A" | ... | "Z"
<digit> ::= "0" | ... | "9"
```

Meaning Notes:
1. `<story>`: Represents the entire story, composed of a custom and a question.
2. `<custom>`: Describes the unbreakable habit. It specifies the initial signals, the sequence of operations, and the asserted equivalences.
3. `<question>`: Describes the scenario for which a regularity is questioned. It specifies the initial signals, the sequence of operations, and the equivalences being asked about.
4. `<signal_list>`: Defines the initial, freely chosen signals for a given part of the story.
5. `<signal_name>`: A name given to an initial signal.
6. `<procedure_list>`: A sequence of operations performed.
7. `<procedure>`: A single operation where one signal is fed "THROUGH" another, producing a new result.
8. `<result_name>`: A name given to the output of a procedure.
9. `<input_signal>`: Can be either an initial signal (`<signal_name>`) or the result of a previous procedure (`<result_name>`).
10. `<equivalence_list>`: A list of pairs of signals that are asserted or questioned to be equivalent.
11. `<equivalence>`: A statement that two signals are equivalent. "EQ" stands for "is equivalent to".
12. `<identifier>`: A general name for signals or results, starting with a letter.

ENCODING:
CUSTOM SIGNALS whistle,hum PROCEDURES Relay1=hum THROUGH hum;Relay2=hum THROUGH Relay1 ASSERT whistle EQ Relay2
QUESTION SIGNALS whistle,hum PROCEDURES Relay1=whistle THROUGH hum ASK whistle EQ Relay1
```
grammar:
```

<story> ::= <custom> <question>

<custom> ::= "CUSTOM" <signal_list> <procedure_list> "ASSERT" <equivalence_list>
<question> ::= "QUESTION" <signal_list> <procedure_list> "ASK" <equivalence_list>

<signal_list> ::= "SIGNALS" <signal_name> ("," <signal_name>)*
<signal_name> ::= <identifier>

<procedure_list> ::= "PROCEDURES" <procedure> (";" <procedure>)*
<procedure> ::= <result_name> "=" <input_signal> "THROUGH" <input_signal>
<result_name> ::= <identifier>
<input_signal> ::= <signal_name> | <result_name>

<equivalence_list> ::= <equivalence> ("AND" <equivalence>)*
<equivalence> ::= <signal_name> "EQ" <input_signal>

<identifier> ::= <letter> (<letter> | <digit>)*
<letter> ::= "a" | ... | "z" | "A" | ... | "Z"
<digit> ::= "0" | ... | "9"
```

Meaning Notes:
1. `<story>`: Represents the entire story, composed of a custom and a question.
2. `<custom>`: Describes the unbreakable habit. It specifies the initial signals, the sequence of operations, and the asserted equivalences.
3. `<question>`: Describes the scenario for which a regularity is questioned. It specifies the initial signals, the sequence of operations, and the equivalences being asked about.
4. `<signal_list>`: Defines the initial, freely chosen signals for a given part of the story.
5. `<signal_name>`: A name given to an initial signal.
6. `<procedure_list>`: A sequence of operations performed.
7. `<procedure>`: A single operation where one signal is fed "THROUGH" another, producing a new result.
8. `<result_name>`: A name given to the output of a procedure.
9. `<input_signal>`: Can be either an initial signal (`<signal_name>`) or the result of a previous procedure (`<result_name>`).
10. `<equivalence_list>`: A list of pairs of signals that are asserted or questioned to be equivalent.
11. `<equivalence>`: A statement that two signals are equivalent. "EQ" stands for "is equivalent to".
12. `<identifier>`: A general name for signals or results, starting with a letter.
encoding:
CUSTOM SIGNALS whistle,hum PROCEDURES Relay1=hum THROUGH hum;Relay2=hum THROUGH Relay1 ASSERT whistle EQ Relay2
QUESTION SIGNALS whistle,hum PROCEDURES Relay1=whistle THROUGH hum ASK whistle EQ Relay1
```

<ResultComparison> ::= <Identifier> "==" <Identifier>

1.  `<Story>`: Represents a complete story, consisting of a custom and a question.
2.  `<Custom>`: Describes the unbreakable habit of the workplace.
3.  `<Question>`: Poses a query about a potential regularity.
4.  `"CUSTOM"`: Keyword indicating the start of the custom description.
5.  `"QUESTION"`: Keyword indicating the start of the question description.
6.  `<InputDeclaration>`: Declares the initial, freely chosen items.
7.  `"INPUTS"`: Keyword indicating the declaration of inputs.
8.  `<IdentifierList>`: A comma-separated list of identifiers for the inputs.
9.  `<Identifier>`: A name for a signal/pigment/etc., composed of letters.
10. `<Letter>`: A lowercase English letter.
11. `<ProcedureList>`: A sequence of operations, separated by semicolons.
12. `<Procedure>`: Assigns the result of an operation to a new identifier.
13. `<Operation>`: Represents the combining action. The identifier on the left is the first item, and the identifier on the right is the second item.
14. `"="`: Assignment operator, assigning the result of an operation to an identifier.
15. `"->"`: Operator representing the combining action. `A -> B` means "A through B" or "A into B".
16. `"ASSERT"`: Keyword indicating that the following comparisons always hold true in the custom.
17. `"ASK"`: Keyword indicating that the following comparisons are being questioned.
18. `<ResultComparisonList>`: A comma-separated list of comparisons between results.
19. `<ResultComparison>`: Compares two identifiers, asserting or asking if they are equivalent.
20. `"=="`: Comparison operator, meaning "is the same as" or "sounds/looks alike".

1. `<story>`: Represents a complete story, consisting of a custom and a question.
2. `<custom>`: Describes the unbreakable habit, including the initial variables, the procedure, and the assertion that always holds.
3. `<question>`: Describes the scenario for which a regularity is questioned, including the initial variables, the procedure, and the query.
4. `"VARS" <var_list>`: Declares the initial set of freely chosen items for the subsequent procedure.
5. `<variable>`: An identifier representing a specific item (e.g., a signal, a pigment, a tea).
6. `"PROC" <step_list>`: Defines a sequence of operations.
7. `<result_var> "=" <action> "(" <operand1> "," <operand2> ")"`: Represents a single combining action. `<result_var>` is the name given to the output of the action. `<action>` is the combining operation. `<operand1>` is the first item, and `<operand2>` is the second item in the combining action. The order matters.
8. `"COMBINE"`: The generic action of combining two things into one.
9. `"ASSERT" <comparison_list>`: States that the listed comparisons always hold true given the preceding procedure and initial variables.
10. `"QUERY" <comparison_list>`: Asks whether the listed comparisons must always hold true given the preceding procedure and initial variables.
11. `<variable> "==" <variable>`: A comparison asserting or querying that two items are equivalent (e.g., "always come out the exact same color," "always taste exactly the same," "always sound exactly alike").

```
<story> ::= <custom> <question>
<custom> ::= "CUSTOM:" <inputs> <procedure> "ASSERT:" <comparison_list>
<question> ::= "QUESTION:" <inputs> <procedure> "ASK:" <comparison_list>

<inputs> ::= "INPUTS:" <input_list>
<input_list> ::= <input_name> | <input_name> "," <input_list>
<input_name> ::= <identifier>

<procedure> ::= "PROCEDURE:" <step_list>
<step_list> ::= <step> | <step> ";" <step_list>
<step> ::= <output_name> "=" <operation>
<operation> ::= <operand> "->" <operand>
<operand> ::= <input_name> | <output_name>

<comparison_list> ::= <comparison> | <comparison> "," <comparison_list>
<comparison> ::= <operand> "==" <operand>

<identifier> ::= <letter> | <identifier> <letter> | <identifier> <digit>
<letter> ::= "a" | ... | "z" | "A" | ... | "Z"
<digit> ::= "0" | ... | "9"
```

Meaning Notes:
1. `<story>`: Represents a complete story, consisting of a custom and a question.
2. `<custom>`: Describes the unbreakable habit, including the initial inputs, the procedure, and the asserted equality.
3. `<question>`: Describes the scenario for which a regularity is questioned, including the initial inputs, the procedure, and the questioned equality.
4. `"CUSTOM:"`, `"QUESTION:"`, `"INPUTS:"`, `"PROCEDURE:"`, `"ASSERT:"`, `"ASK:"`: Keywords marking different sections of the story.
5. `<input_list>`: A comma-separated list of identifiers representing the initial, freely chosen items.
6. `<input_name>`: An identifier referring to one of the initial inputs.
7. `<step_list>`: A semicolon-separated list of procedural steps.
8. `<step>`: A single operation that produces a named result.
9. `<output_name>`: An identifier referring to the result of a step (e.g., "Batch1", "Relay2").
10. `<operation>`: Represents the combining action. The `->` symbol indicates the order of combination. The left operand is the "first" thing, and the right operand is the "second" thing.
11. `<operand>`: Can be either an initial input or the result of a previous step.
12. `<comparison_list>`: A comma-separated list of comparisons.
13. `<comparison>`: Two operands separated by `==` indicates that the two things always come out the same.
14. `<identifier>`: A sequence of letters and digits used for naming inputs and outputs.

ENCODING:
CUSTOM:
INPUTS: whistle,hum,chirp
PROCEDURE:
Relay1 = hum -> hum;
Relay2 = Relay1 -> hum;
Relay3 = Relay2 -> chirp;
Relay4 = Relay3 -> hum
ASSERT: whistle == Relay4
QUESTION:
INPUTS: whistle,hum,chirp,buzz
PROCEDURE:
Relay1 = whistle -> hum;
Relay2 = buzz -> hum;
Relay3 = buzz -> Relay2;
Relay4 = chirp -> Relay3
ASK: Relay1 == Relay4
grammar:
```
<story> ::= <custom> <question>

<input_name> ::= <identifier>

<procedure> ::= "PROCEDURE:" <step_list>
<step_list> ::= <step> | <step> ";" <step_list>
<step> ::= <output_name> "=" <operation>
<operation> ::= <operand> "->" <operand>
<operand> ::= <input_name> | <output_name>

<comparison_list> ::= <comparison> | <comparison> "," <comparison_list>
<comparison> ::= <operand> "==" <operand>

<identifier> ::= <letter> | <identifier> <letter> | <identifier> <digit>
<letter> ::= "a" | ... | "z" | "A" | ... | "Z"
<digit> ::= "0" | ... | "9"
```
